era_context: The late 14th century in Europe was marked by the aftermath of the Black Death (1347–1353), which had killed 30–60% of Europe's population. The Hundred Years' War between England and France continued with intermittent campaigns. The Ottoman Empire was expanding in the Balkans and Anatolia. In East Asia, the Ming Dynasty (founded 1368) was consolidating control over China after overthrowing the Mongol Yuan. The Papacy remained in Avignon, and the Western Church faced growing institutional criticism.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records from this period)
Americas (no European contact; indigenous records limited)

events.title: Battle of Bileća (Ottoman-Serbian Conflict)
events.description: In 1371, Ottoman forces under Murad I defeated Serbian forces at the Battle of Bileća (also known as the Battle of the Maritsa or related conflicts in the Balkans). This battle was part of the broader Ottoman expansion into the Balkans and represented a significant Ottoman victory over Serbian resistance. The battle contributed to the Ottoman consolidation of power in the region and the gradual decline of Serbian independence. The exact date and details of this battle are somewhat unclear in sources, but it is generally placed in the early 1370s as part of Murad I's systematic conquest of the Balkans.

sources.name: Serbian and Byzantine chronicles (contemporary or near-contemporary)

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Bileća is attested in later Ottoman chronicles and Serbian sources, but the precise date is uncertain. It is generally placed in the early 1370s, but dating is approximate within ±3 years. Some sources conflate this battle with other Ottoman-Serbian conflicts of the period.
cross_references: Part of Ottoman expansion in the Balkans (1362–1389)

events.title: Ming Dynasty Consolidates Control Over Yunnan
events.description: In 1371, the Ming Dynasty completed or consolidated its military control over Yunnan Province in southwestern China, which had been a center of Yuan Dynasty resistance. The Ming conquest of Yunnan was part of the broader consolidation of Ming power over all of China. The region had been ruled by the Duan family and other local powers, and the Ming brought it under direct imperial control. This conquest was significant for establishing Ming territorial integrity and eliminating remaining Yuan loyalist strongholds.
key_figures: Duan family and local Yunnan rulers

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)
Historiographic consensus on Ming consolidation of Yunnan

events.certainty_note: The general timeline of Ming conquest of Yunnan is well-established, with the conquest largely completed by 1371. However, the precise date of final consolidation is approximate within ±1 year.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Bileća is sometimes conflated with other Ottoman-Serbian conflicts of the early 1370s, and its precise date and significance are disputed by modern scholarship. Some sources place it in 1371, while others date it to 1373 or other years in the early 1370s. The battle's significance is also debated—while it was an Ottoman victory, it was not necessarily a decisive turning point in Ottoman-Serbian relations.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1371 is sparse and fragmented. Balkan events are documented in later Ottoman chronicles and Serbian sources, but precise dating is uncertain. East Asian events (Ming consolidation) are well-documented in Chinese sources but lack specific annual detail for 1371. The year is not marked by major turning points in any region, making it a relatively quiet year in global history.

graph_edges.note: Ottoman victories in the early 1370s, including Bileća, contributed to the Ottoman dominance that culminated in the Battle of Kosovo in 1389
Ming consolidation of Yunnan was part of the broader Ming consolidation process of the 1370s
